stories.title: Nomura Prices Largest Single-Bank SASB CMBS in Two Years
stories.excerpt: As it approaches its first birthday, Nomura Securities’ commercial real estate lending platform has an extra reason to celebrate: It just sealed the largest sole-bank single-asset, single-borrower commercial mortgage-backed securities (SASB CMBS) deal in almost two years. The $719 million KELR 2026-MF deal is backed by 12 multifamily properties and one student housing asset, all owned by Keller Investment Properties. Nomura is the sole lender in the floating-rate, interest-only deal, which has a two-year term with three one-year extension options. The deal marks not only the largest single-bank SASB since August 2024, but it also places it among the largest single-bank SASB deals since 2020 — not too shabby for a platform that launched only last summer. Further, the transaction received a strong response from investors and was multiple times oversubscribed, sources said. Newmark brokered the financing, and the AAA bonds — rated by Fitch Ratings and Moody’s — priced at 135 basis points over the Secured Overnight Financing Rate earlier this week. The portfolio collateral is spread across Utah, Nevada and Arizona, and totals 3,321 units, including the sole student housing property in the portfolio, which accommodates students at Utah Valley University. Keller acquired the portfolio between 2001 and 2022, and has since invested $46 million in capital improvements, according to Fitch’s presale report. The CMBS loan proceeds will refinance $696 million in existing debt on the portfolio and also fund $22.2 million in closing-related costs and reserves. The transaction may have been a first for Nomura, but it was also one for Keller Investment Properties, based just outside of Salt Lake City, which historically has been a borrower of agency debt, sources said. As such, KELR 2026-MF marks the company’s SASB CMBS debut. The deal closed in just over 60 days, which one source described as “a land speed record category for a first-time CMBS borrower.” “We are pleased with the ou
bullets: Nomura closes $719M single-bank SASB CMBS on 12 multifamily + 1 student housing asset from Keller Investment Properties.
Largest sole-bank SASB since August 2024; deal heavily oversubscribed, signaling strong investor appetite for floating-rate MF paper.
Two-year term with extension options; reflects viable securitization path for institutional multifamily portfolios amid tighter lending.
stories.summary: Nomura Securities' CRE lending platform closed a $719 million single-asset, single-borrower CMBS backed by 13 properties (12 multifamily, 1 student housing) owned by Keller Investment Properties. The floating-rate, interest-only deal carries a two-year term with three one-year extension options and received heavy oversubscription from investors. It marks the largest sole-bank SASB since August 2024 and among the largest since 2020.
stories.impact: Strong oversubscription signals investor confidence in multifamily securitization despite macro uncertainty, easing refinance availability for stabilized portfolios. Developers with performing assets now have a clearer exit path via CMBS, reducing refinance risk and unlocking capital for new acquisitions and starts.

stories.title: No, the Pfizer Near-Disaster Won’t Slow New York’s Conversion Wave
stories.excerpt: New York City is relying, in part, on more than 16,000 units planned via office-to-residential conversions to ease its enduring housing crisis. So, when the country’s largest conversion, sitting in the heart of Midtown, starts to buckle, it’s not taken lightly. The startling structural emergency at MetroLoft and David Werner Real Estate Investment’s Pfizer building conversion in early July prompted a swift response from the city. Reports of two buckling columns prompted officials to cordon off 40th to 45th streets between First and Third avenues in the immediate aftermath. Several buildings were evacuated, including a school where roughly 400 children were relocated. In the following weeks, after temporary supports secured the building, the New York City Department of Buildings (DOB) initiated citywide construction safety sweeps, including checks on projects linked to MetroLoft’s contractor, Barone Steel Fabricators, Gothamist reported. A full investigation of the emergency is ongoing, but the final verdict will likely come down to the two columns, and the heavy load above them. The real estate industry is largely in agreement that the Pfizer emergency was a freak accident. Yet, when a conversion of such fame and magnitude — in which 19 stories and 1,600 apartments are being added to the existing structure — causes such disruption, anxiety is understandable. New regulations from the city, and accompanying cost overruns, may now be the industry’s real worry. Is it enough to spook the market? Development, real estate and housing advocates sure hope not. Conversion projects, like any major development, experience pressures to stay on time and on budget, intensified by an inflationary, volatile capital markets environment and heightened construction costs in the Big Apple. Insiders argue that the scare is unlikely to derail New York’s office conversion boom, given the investor appetite and political will behind multifamily housing. New York City boasts the largest conve
bullets: Pfizer building conversion (MetroLoft/David Werner REIT) in Midtown NYC halted July due to two buckling steel columns; 40-45 streets cordoned off.
NYC counts on 16,000+ conversion units to address housing crisis; structural failure triggered citywide DOB safety sweeps on MetroLoft contractor Barone Steel.
Incident unlikely to derail conversion pipeline; investigation ongoing but does not signal broader market pullback.
stories.summary: A structural emergency at MetroLoft and David Werner REIT's Pfizer building conversion in Midtown Manhattan prompted the evacuation of multiple buildings and a citywide construction safety sweep. Two buckling columns required temporary bracing and triggered an investigation by NYC's Department of Buildings, including audits of related contractor Barone Steel. The incident affected blocks between 40th–45th streets and First–Third avenues.
stories.impact: Temporary setback on the largest office-to-residential conversion in the U.S., but unlikely to materially slow NYC's 16,000-unit conversion pipeline. Increased DOB scrutiny will raise steel fabrication and QA costs on future conversions, tightening underwriting margins. Developers should expect longer permit reviews and potentially higher contingencies for structural certifications in Manhattan and citywide.

stories.title: Inflation Could Stay Higher: What it Means for CRE
stories.excerpt: The June Consumer Price Index offered some good news: inflation dropped to 3.5%, driven by lower oil and gas prices. However, that state of affairs is unlikely to last, especially as tensions in the Middle East are heating up again, suggesting the U.S. isn’t free from the elevated inflation cycle just yet. In a recently released video, “Inflation Risk: Headwind or Opportunity for CRE?” Vice President John Chang discussed the realities of inflation and its potential impact on commercial real estate. First, the ongoing Middle East conflict could lead to an “extended cycle of elevated prices, which, in turn, could sustain inflationary pressure,” he said. It could also signal a higher probability of a federal interest rate hike by the end of the year. Second, other inflation metrics, like food, housing and medical care, have comparatively low inflation numbers in the 2 to mid-3% range. Energy prices, which affect transportation costs, could lead to ongoing higher headline inflation. Third, this isn’t unexpected news for commercial real estate investors. “In large part, the risk of potential rate increases is already baked into Treasury and lending rates, and because they’re already baked in, the cost of debt capital should remain relatively stable,” Chang said. Fourth, even in an extended inflation cycle, commercial real estate is one of the few investments that can offer some inflation resistance. “Despite elevated uncertainty, despite the Middle East conflict, and despite tariff-driven headwinds, all four of the major property types are generating positive space demand,” Chang said. Based on preliminary reports from Q2, office properties reported a ninth consecutive quarter of positive net absorption, while apartment demand pushed vacancy rates lower. At the same time, retail and industrial space had relatively stable vacancy rates. Finally, inflation could decline, and economic momentum could strengthen if a resolution to the Middle East conflict is reached. That cou
bullets: June CPI dropped to 3.5% via lower oil/gas; but Middle East tensions risk sustained elevated energy prices and headline inflation.
Risk of year-end fed rate hike exists; however, forward rates already price in higher-for-longer scenario, stabilizing debt costs.
Food, housing, medical inflation remain modest (2–3.5%); energy price volatility remains primary inflation wildcard for CRE ops.
stories.summary: June CPI fell to 3.5%, driven by lower energy prices, but ongoing Middle East tensions could sustain elevated oil costs and headline inflation. While a potential year-end fed rate hike is possible, forward rates have largely priced in the higher-for-longer scenario. Food, housing, and medical inflation remain comparatively contained at 2–3.5%, minimizing pressure from non-energy sources.
stories.impact: Debt capital costs already reflect inflation expectations, so near-term financing conditions likely remain stable despite rate-hike risks. However, sustained energy prices will keep construction labor and material transport costs elevated, pressuring development feasibility and cap-rate compression in high-rent markets. Developers should lock in forward debt at current rates and budget for sustained input cost inflation through 2027.

stories.title: Office’s New Purpose: Affiliation
stories.excerpt: In 2025, global office utilization reported its largest annual gain in four years. But employees aren’t returning simply because they’re being told to. Lenny Beaudoin According to CBRE’s 2026 “Global Workplace & Occupancy Insights” survey report, organizations that design their office spaces for collaboration, culture and connection are experiencing the strongest attendance gains. Office utilization rose to 53% in 2025 from 38% a year earlier. Peak utilization reached 80%, while average occupancy climbed to 111%, reflecting a higher level of desk sharing and more flexible workplace strategies. “We’re seeing the best attendance gains where organizations make the office a more purposeful place to be,” Lenny Beaudoin, CBRE’s executive managing director of workplace, design and occupancy, told Connect CRE. Beaudoin, who co-authored the report with CBRE colleague Susan Wasmund, said organizations are increasingly measuring office performance by what the workplace supports rather than how full it is. “It’s being judged by what it enables,” he said. “The next generation of workplace strategy is about affiliation, collaboration, culture and performance.” Collaboration Replaces Solo Work The report suggests that the office’s primary purpose has shifted from providing individual work space to fostering interaction. Among survey respondents, 93% said that colleague collaboration was a main reason for coming into the office, followed by in-person meetings (90%) and social interaction and team building (89%). That shift is also impacting office layouts. Between 2021 and 2025, space dedicated to individual workstations fell from 56% to 35% of the workplace, while collaborative areas expanded from 12% to 56%. What about amenities? The report said that vacancy in prime office buildings that offer plentiful amenities is “measurable lower than the broader market.” More important than the latest, shinies
bullets: Office utilization jumped to 53% in 2025 from 38% YoY; peak occupancy reached 80%, average 111% via desk-sharing.
Strongest attendance gains in orgs designing offices for collaboration, culture, connection—not mandate-driven returns.
Next-gen workplace judged by 'affiliation, collaboration, culture, performance,' not square footage occupancy metrics.
stories.summary: Global office utilization posted its largest annual gain in four years in 2025, rising to 53% from 38%, with peak occupancy reaching 80% and average occupancy hitting 111% via increased desk-sharing. CBRE's 2026 survey shows organizations that design offices for collaboration and culture experience the strongest attendance gains, not those relying on mandates. The industry is shifting from occupancy-centric metrics to assessing workplace value by what it enables.
stories.impact: Demonstrates that well-designed, purpose-built office still drives in-person use and justifies premium rents in major metros. Multifamily developers should note: companies keeping office real estate are those with strong culture and collaboration needs, signaling continued tenant demand in urban submarkets with mixed-use, hospitality-grade amenities. However, poorly designed or Class-B suburban office will continue to lose tenants to conversion or obsolescence; no multi-family demand lift from weak office stock.

stories.title: How AI Is Transforming Utility Real Estate and Workplace Portfolios
stories.excerpt: Artificial intelligence is helping utility companies improve customer service and better manage power grids. According to a recent JLL report, “Optimizing Beyond the Grid: How AI is Revolutionizing Utility Facilities and Workplaces,” the technology is doing a whole lot more than that. “Leveraging AI in their real estate functions can help utilities cut costs, advance sustainability and attract and retain talent,” the report said. “By harnessing the capabilities of generative AI, utilities can transform their owned and leased real estate into self-optimizing assets that drive business performance.” Today’s utility companies are dealing with increasing electricity demand, in part due to expanding data centers. A Gartner forecast said that 40% of power and utility organizations will deploy AI-driven operators in control rooms by 2027. Because utilities are about more than power grids (their real estate portfolios include corporate offices, operations centers, warehouses, maintenance buildings and substations), the report said that consolidating all facility and asset data into centralized platforms gives AI the information it needs to identify inefficiencies and recommend improvements. “Utilities are leveraging AI to eliminate fragmentation and turn their facilities into intelligent portfolios,” said Emily Roman, executive director of technology advisory for industrials at JLL. Reliance on Prevention The report said that predictive maintenance is one of AI’s more important tasks. The technology puts teams into a proactive stance, as it can continuously monitor equipment condition and catch potential errors before they become huge problems. Teams can focus on maintenance rather than reacting to breakdowns. Although predictive maintenance requires upfront investment, JLL estimates the strategy can generate returns of up to 545% over time through lower capital replacement costs, improved equipment efficiency and reduced energy consumption. Real Estate Planning
bullets: Utilities deploying AI to optimize corporate offices, operations centers, warehouses, maintenance facilities, substations; 40% expected to use AI-driven operators by 2027.
Utilities driving 40% of new data center electricity demand; AI adoption helps identify facility inefficiencies across fragmented real estate portfolios.
AI consolidating facility/asset data into centralized platforms to recommend cost, sustainability, and talent-retention improvements.
stories.summary: Utilities are adopting AI across their real estate portfolios—including offices, operations centers, warehouses, and substations—to cut costs, improve sustainability, and attract talent. Gartner forecasts 40% of power and utility organizations will deploy AI-driven control-room operators by 2027. By consolidating fragmented facility and asset data into centralized AI platforms, utilities can identify inefficiencies and optimize their owned and leased real estate.
stories.impact: Rising electricity demand from data centers (utilities' largest load growth driver) is reshaping utility real estate strategy and consolidation trends. Developers should track which utilities are consolidating or shedding office/operations campuses due to AI-driven efficiency gains; opportunity for acquisition of utility-owned land or real estate in strategic metros. Additionally, multifamily projects near substations or data-center hubs may face increased infrastructure costs or zoning friction as utilities upgrade power delivery.

stories.title: Forest Development, Kast Construction face off in legal battle over Lake Park condo
stories.excerpt: Forest Development and Kast Construction are going head-to-head in a legal battle over Nautilus 220 condominiums in Lake Park.  The condo’s developer and builder are suing each other for alleged contract breach, incomplete work and failure to pay millions of dollars owed, The Palm Beach Post reported.  Forest Development filed a lawsuit July 13 in Palm Beach County circuit court, accusing Kast of not completing work and alleging that the builder’s improper supervision of its subcontractors resulted in water damage and unnecessary delays. The lawsuit also claims Kast refused to release liens on sold properties, hindering Forest’s ability to close […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Forest Development sues Kast Construction for incomplete work, water damage, delayed project, and retained liens on sold units at Nautilus 220, Lake Park.
Kast countersues for $11.4M unpaid progress payments; claims Forest submitted late/inaccurate change orders disrupting workflow.
Subcontractors have filed $2.9M in liens; legal impasse blocks Forest's ability to close sales and clear titles.
stories.summary: Developer Forest Development and builder Kast Construction are locked in mutual litigation over the Nautilus 220 condominium in Lake Park, Florida. Forest alleges incomplete work, water damage, and retained liens preventing sales closings; Kast claims $11.4 million in unpaid progress payments and disputes over change orders and timeline delays. Subcontractors have filed an additional $2.9 million in liens, freezing the project's ability to deliver units to buyers.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily supply; this is a condo project. However, the dispute highlights construction cost escalation, change-order dysfunction, and contractor cash-flow stress in South Florida's competitive build environment. Multifamily developers in the region should strengthen contract language, escrow provisions, and lien-release protocols with builders to avoid similar standoffs.

stories.title: Policy Pro: Developers back ballot bid to speed up construction permits
stories.excerpt: This story gives you a peek at the content coming to our new platform, TRD Policy Pro. Sign up to get early access here. Hello, let’s get into today’s news at the intersection of policy and real estate: In this edition we mention: Gotham Organization President of Development Brian Kelly, Monadnock Development President Kirk Goodrich, Open New York Executive Director Annemarie Gray, the Department of Finance, the Department of Buildings and others. We Heard Have a tip or feedback? Reach me at caroline.spivack@therealdeal.com.  The Catch-Up Imposing new union-requested wage mandates on office-to-residential conversions would increase labor costs, making fewer projects […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC developers back ballot measure to speed construction permitting; union-requested wage mandates on office-to-residential conversions cited as cost-driver.
Gotham Organization, Monadnock Development, Open New York advocate faster permitting; wage mandates could make fewer conversion projects financially viable.
NYC lagging opportunity-zone disclosure; HUD fair-housing enforcement rules under federal challenge.
stories.summary: NYC developers are backing a ballot initiative to accelerate construction permits, with Gotham Organization, Monadnock Development, and Open New York voicing concern that union-requested wage mandates on office-to-residential conversions will increase labor costs and reduce project viability. New York remains one of 15 states that have not publicized its next opportunity-zone designation plan. Separately, HUD faces a federal lawsuit over rollbacks to fair-housing grant enforcement.
stories.impact: Wage-mandate proposals directly threaten the economics of NYC's office-to-residential conversion pipeline, which the city depends on for ~16,000 units to address housing shortage. If mandates pass, expect fewer conversions to pencil, shifting demand to new-build multifamily in outer boroughs/suburbs with lower labor costs. Permitting acceleration is critical to offset wage-cost inflation; developers should monitor ballot outcomes and advocate for carve-outs or phase-ins for conversions to protect pro-forma viability.

stories.title: Fashion Nova Expands L.A. Footprint With 133K-SF Warehouse Deal
stories.excerpt: Fashion Nova is expanding its Southern California logistics side with another industrial lease in Santa Fe Springs. The Los Angeles-based fast-fashion retailer signed a lease to take over the 132,888-square-foot warehouse at 12301 Hawkins Street, according to CoStar. It increases Fashion Nova’s warehouse footprint in Santa Fe Springs by about 33 percent. New York-based real estate investment manager Clarion Partners owns the property in the Heritage Springs Business Park. It includes 12,004 square feet of office space on a 5.4-acre site, according to a JLL marketing brochure. The recently renovated facility features 30-foot clear heights, 18 dock-high doors, two ground-level doors and 175 parking spaces. The owner did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The deal adds to leasing momentum picking up across L.A. County’s industrial market. Leasing activity reached 15.7 million square feet during the second quarter, up 40 percent compared to last year, according to CBRE. The market also posted 2.8 million square feet of positive net absorption — its third consecutive positive quarter after 10 straight quarters of negative absorption. Vacancy declined 22 basis points since the first quarter to 5 percent, although landlords continue to face falling rents. Average asking rents dropped to $1.19 per square foot per month, down 7 percent from a year prior, and down 32.4 percent from the second quarter of 2023. The Mid-Counties submarket, which includes Santa Fe Springs, recorded 1.1 million square feet of positive absorption during the quarter and an average asking rate of $1.23 per square foot. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Fashion Nova expands Santa Fe Springs warehouse footprint by 33% via 132,888-SF lease at 12301 Hawkins Street; owned by Clarion Partners.
LA County industrial leasing hit 15.7M SF in Q2 2025, up 40% YoY; positive net absorption reflects strong ecommerce/logistics demand.
Facility features 30-ft clear heights, 18 dock doors, recently renovated—prime spec for fast-fashion logistics.
stories.summary: Fashion Nova leased a 132,888-square-foot warehouse in Santa Fe Springs, increasing its local logistics footprint by 33%. The recently renovated facility, owned by Clarion Partners, offers 30-foot clear heights, 18 dock-high doors, and premium specifications suited to fast-fashion fulfillment. LA County industrial leasing activity reached 15.7 million square feet in Q2 2025, up 40% year-over-year.
stories.impact: Strong industrial leasing momentum in LA County (up 40% YoY) confirms robust ecommerce and omnichannel fulfillment demand, supporting land values and spec industrial development. Multifamily developers in LA County should factor industrial cluster growth into site selection; submarkets with strong industrial absorption (Santa Fe Springs, Vernon, Long Beach) will see sustained labor inflow and rental demand from warehouse/logistics workers, supporting multifamily rents in adjacent areas.

stories.title: Beyond the Flight to Quality: The Office Questions That Aren’t Being Asked
stories.excerpt: Jim Adler The conversation surrounding office real estate often gets stuck on the same-old, same-old: remote work mandates, flight-to-quality trends, and peak interest rates. But beneath those surface headlines, industry leaders are tracking shift-driving dynamics that haven’t yet hit the main stage. Connect CRE reached out to top minds across brokerage, investment, debt placement, and research to ask a simple question: What is the one office question the market isn’t asking right now? From AI infrastructure and workplace friendships to property tax lag and cash-flow valuations, here is what they say should be on your radar. BROKERAGE & ADVISORY Is Your Building AI-Ready? Jim Adler, Executive Vice President, NAI Hiffman Amanda Kross “Much of today’s conversation centers on the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence. But we may be overlooking a more immediate question for commercial real estate: Is my workplace AI-ready? As companies evaluate office space in the years ahead, they may increasingly assess a building’s ‘AI score’ that will reflect two factors. First, whether a building can support a technology-enabled workforce, and second, whether it helps companies attract and retain the talent needed to develop and use AI. For years, office owners have competed by adding better amenities. The next in-demand offering just might be called ‘AI-optimized.'” The Shift in Geographic Footprints Amanda Kross, Executive Managing Director, Financial Services Division, JLL Anna Squires Levine “At a macro level, AI is enabling companies to be increasingly selective about which roles belong in which talent hubs, rather than simply chasing talent to their existing locations. Geopolitical shifts, supply chain realignment, and changing workforce dynamics are quietly causing companies to reassess their geographic footprint. But most of the office conversation hasn’t caught up to that yet.” “Friendship P
bullets: Office industry overlooks AI-readiness question: whether buildings support tech-enabled workforce and attract/retain AI talent.
Emerging metric: buildings' 'AI score' based on tech infrastructure and talent-attraction capability—next frontier beyond traditional amenities.
Deeper office dynamics (property tax lag, cash-flow valuations, workplace friendships) underexamined by mainstream flight-to-quality narrative.
stories.summary: Industry leaders identify overlooked office dynamics beyond remote work and flight-to-quality, including an emerging 'AI score' metric assessing whether buildings support technology-enabled workforces and talent attraction. Other underexamined factors include property tax lag effects, cash-flow valuation methodology, and workplace relationship dynamics. AI infrastructure and talent retention are positioned as next-generation competitive differentiators for office landlords.
stories.impact: Signals long-term office demand will bifurcate: premium, tech-enabled Class-A space in hub metros (NYC, SF, Austin, Boston) will hold rents and attract corporate anchor tenants; Class-B/C and suburban office will continue to bleed to conversion or obsolescence. Multifamily developers in tech-hub metros should prioritize mixed-use and proximity to AI/tech employment clusters to capture high-wage renter demand. Conversion opportunities remain strongest in secondary office markets where supply vastly exceeds demand for enterprise-grade space.

stories.title: Systima Closes $153M Tax-Exempt CMBS Affordable Housing Deal
stories.excerpt: A private-label securitization closed by Systima Capital Management for a portfolio of nearly 1,300 subsided apartments underscores the potential for use of the commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) market in affordable housing deals. Systima closed the $153 million tax-exempt affordable housing bond deal through the Public Finance Authority securitized by a pool of loans on seven properties by the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) program. The transaction, which utilized tax-exempt CMBS debt for the 1,272-unit portfolio across Wisconsin, Illinois, Florida, Tennessee and Texas were “significantly oversubscribed” with more than $1.2 billion in orders from 19 institutional investors, according to Systima. Ryan Paszczykowski, head of structured investing at Systima, said the tax-exempt CMBS offering in the deal is poised to become more mainstream going forward thanks to strong investor appetite. J.P. Morgan led the transaction as the lead underwriter with Wells Fargo as co-manager, while Systima retained the subordinate Class B certificates. “I think institutional investors are starting to realize the credit protections that currently exist in affordable housing and the significant need for affordable housing,” Paszczykowski told CO. “I think the market is going to grow going forward.” Paszczykowski noted that tax-exempt CMBS financings have been used for affordable housing projects in the past but were typically “one off” that banks executed to free up their balance sheets. He said Systima is seeking to become one of the first dedicated managers to bring this capital markets strategy to the forefront as an avenue to deliver high-risk adjusted returns to investors. The original loan proceeds were used to fund acquisition, construction, rehabilitation and preservation of the affordable housing communities geared toward families earning at or below 60 percent of area median income. The exact locations and names of the individual properties
bullets: Systima Capital closes $153M tax-exempt CMBS affordable housing securitization backed by 1,272 LIHTC units across WI, IL, FL, TN, TX.
Deal oversubscribed 7.8x ($1.2B in orders from 19 institutional investors); signals mainstream adoption of tax-exempt CMBS for affordable housing.
Portfolio across five states demonstrates geographic diversification and institutional investor confidence in LIHTC portfolio securitization.
stories.summary: Systima Capital Management closed a $153 million tax-exempt CMBS securitization for a portfolio of 1,272 LIHTC-backed affordable rental units across Wisconsin, Illinois, Florida, Tennessee, and Texas. The transaction was oversubscribed 7.8 times, with $1.2 billion in demand from 19 institutional investors. JP Morgan led the underwriting, highlighting growing investor appetite for tax-exempt CMBS structures in affordable housing.
stories.impact: Tax-exempt CMBS emerging as viable exit and refi pathway for stabilized LIHTC portfolios, expanding capital availability and improving returns on affordable housing development. Developers with performing LIHTC projects now have lower-cost refinance option via tax-exempt securitization, freeing equity for new acquisitions. Expect increased competition for LIHTC allocations and seasoned affordable housing portfolios as institutional capital shifts toward securitization-ready assets.

stories.title: M&T RCC Provides $141M Bridge Loan to Newly Built Turtle Bay Apartments
stories.excerpt: M&T Realty Capital Corporation (M&T RCC) closed on a $141.4-million bridge loan for Anagram Turtle Bay, a newly constructed 194-unit multifamily community located at 300 E. 50th Stt. in Manhattan’s Turtle Bay neighborhood. The financing was provided to an ownership group led by Global Holdings, MAG Partners and Safanad. The three-year bridge loan, which includes an extension option, refinanced the property’s construction financing and is designed to support the asset through the final stages of lease-up and stabilization before a planned transition to long-term permanent financing. “Bridge financing plays an increasingly important role in today’s market, particularly for newly delivered, high-quality multifamily assets that are approaching stabilization,” said Joe Pizzutelli, M&T RCC head of national production, “Anagram Turtle Bay represents exactly the type of opportunity where bridge capital can create value by providing flexibility and certainty of execution while the property completes its lease-up and transitions to permanent financing.” The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post M&T RCC Provides $141M Bridge Loan to Newly Built Turtle Bay Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: M&T RCC closes $141.4M bridge loan for Anagram Turtle Bay, 194-unit new-build in Manhattan's Turtle Bay neighborhood (E. 50th St.).
Three-year bridge with extension option; owned by Global Holdings, MAG Partners, Safanad; supports lease-up and stabilization prior to permanent refi.
Bridge financing de facto standard for newly stabilized MF assets; provides flexibility and certainty during lease-up completion phase.
stories.summary: M&T Realty Capital Corporation provided a $141.4 million bridge loan for Anagram Turtle Bay, a newly constructed 194-unit multifamily community in Manhattan's Turtle Bay neighborhood. The three-year bridge, arranged for an ownership group led by Global Holdings, MAG Partners, and Safanad, refinances construction financing and carries an extension option. Bridge capital is positioned as essential support during final lease-up and stabilization before transition to long-term permanent financing.
stories.impact: M&T's bridge deployment underscores ongoing capital availability for newly delivered, high-quality multifamily assets in Manhattan—a positive signal for development feasibility and exit certainty in prime urban markets. Bridge financing allows developers to hold stabilizing assets without aggressive permanent-debt pricing, preserving upside. Expect bridge products to remain core financing tool for new-construction completions in gateway markets through 2026–2027.

stories.title: Todd Rosenberg’s Pebb Capital Buys South Beach Office Building
stories.excerpt: Todd Rosenberg’s Pebb Capital has purchased the Uno office building in South Beach, as boutique financial firms vie for outposts in Miami. The seven-story building features about 100,000 square feet of leasable space and 106 covered parking spots at 119 Washington Avenue, between First and Second streets, in the upscale South of Fifth neighborhood. While the sale price remains unclear, it’s likely north of $69 million since Acore Capital provided a loan for that amount, property records show. A representative of Pebb Capital has not responded to a request for comment. Property records show an entity named Yantra 119, managed by Longstead at The Corcoran Group’s Cyril Bijaoui, has owned the building since 2011. Bijaoui, who manages and leases the property, declined to comment. During the pandemic, the seller renovated the interiors of the building, which was constructed in 2002. Earlier this month, the entity launched a construction project to add about 10,000 square feet of office space by converting a terrace. The sale comes as South Beach is seeing an uptick in new Class A office buildings, with demand coming from wealthy executives who want to work near their homes. The Fifth, a nearby boutique building backed by Google billionaire Eric Schmidt, is nearing completion after securing a $47.3 million construction loan in 2024. Hedge fund J. Goldman & Company signed a 10,000-square-foot lease, and Sant Ambroeus will open a 7,000-square-foot restaurant on the ground floor. Across the street, the same development team, Sumaida + Khurana and Bizzi + Bilgili, have another luxury office project, dubbed 1100 Fifth Street, in the pipeline. Further north in Sunset Harbour, private equity giant Ares Management opened a 10,500-square-foot office at the five-story Eighteen Sunset development, which was completed in 2024. Boca Raton-based Pebb Capital is making inroads in Miami. In January, the firm purchased a mixed-use building in the Miami Design District for $73 mi
bullets: Todd Rosenberg's Pebb Capital purchases Uno office building in South Beach (119 Washington Avenue); ~100K SF, 106 parking spaces.
Sale price likely >$69M (Acore loan amount); seller added 10K SF via terrace conversion prior to sale.
South Beach office attracting financial-services tenants; Pebb bet on boutique finance firms expanding Miami presence.
stories.summary: Pebb Capital, led by Todd Rosenberg, acquired the Uno office building in South Beach, a seven-story, 100,000-square-foot property at 119 Washington Avenue. Acore Capital provided a $69 million loan, suggesting a sale price north of that figure. The previous owner, managed by Longstead/Corcoran's Cyril Bijaoui since 2011, had recently renovated interiors and added ~10,000 square feet via terrace conversion.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact on multifamily; office acquisition in a declining asset class. However, signals that South Beach waterfront office is being repositioned for finance/boutique tenants rather than converted to residential, suggesting owner confidence in office fundamentals in Miami's high-rent neighborhoods. Multifamily developers should note: South Beach rents and condo prices remain elevated despite office supply—strong household demand from financial-services workers and tourists buttress overall market strength.

stories.title: Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key, Live Local high-rise among $420M-plus South Florida construction permits for week ending July 22, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest permit issued was a $240 million new construction permit for 1 Island Drive in Miami, where Swire Properties is developing The Residences at Mandarin Oriental, Miami and Mandarin Oriental Hotel on Brickell Key. The former Mandarin Oriental Hotel towers on the site were demolished via controlled implosion in April. The redevelopment will include condos and a hotel, totaling about 1.35 million square feet. Job Filing Number: BD23028760001B001 📊 New Building: The second-largest permit was $95 million for 114 Northeast 22nd Street in Miami, where New Sense LLC, an affiliate of Argentina-based HA Emprendimientos, is developing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Swire Properties issues $240M permit for Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key redevelopment; 1.35M SF with condos, hotel on former hotel site (demolished April).
New Sense LLC gets $95M permit for 36-story, 320+ unit Live Local Act mixed-use at 114 NE 22nd, Miami; funded by $111M construction loan from S3 Capital.
South Florida permits week ending July 22: $420M+; Lore Development and Element Development adding 112 studio units on NW 15th in 8-story mixed-use.
stories.summary: South Florida issued over $420 million in construction permits for the week ending July 22, 2026. Swire Properties received a $240 million permit for the Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key redevelopment (1.35 million SF with condos and hotel). New Sense LLC secured a $95 million permit for the 36-story Sense 22 project (320+ units, Live Local Act) with $111 million in construction financing from S3 Capital. Lore Development and Element Development obtained a $21.3 million permit for a 112-unit, eight-story mixed-use project.
stories.impact: Strong permit velocity in Miami signals continued confidence in mixed-use and residential development despite macro headwinds. Live Local Act and tax-increment financing supporting new residential supply in health districts and infill areas. Multifamily developers should track Live Local Act project pipeline in Miami and Jacksonville—favorable tax treatment and development rights are driving supply and will compress Class-A rents over 2–3 years. Current window for premium new-build absorption; expect softening by 2028.

stories.title: UrbanStreet targets former Motorola office for residential conversion
stories.excerpt: UrbanStreet Group is planning a residential conversion of an atrium office building that used to house Motorola, as part of Schaumburg’s the District at Veridian development. During an informal presentation with village officials, UrbanStreet managing partner Bob Burk said that converting the six-story, 360,200-square-foot building would be a “poster child” for redevelopment creativity, according to the Chicago Daily Herald. The project also includes the construction of a sister building that would reach as high as four stories, giving space for the complex to have around 300 units. 18 row houses will connect the complex to D.R. Horton’s Northgate at Veridian […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: UrbanStreet converts 360K-SF six-story ex-Motorola office into ~300-unit apartment complex at Schaumburg's District at Veridian megadevelopment.
District at Veridian $1B mixed-use project includes sister 4-story building, 18 row houses, retail, parking, 150-key hotel; first phase construction nearing completion.
UrbanStreet locked $94M construction loan from Bank OZK in 2025 for first phase; phase-one cost ~$175M, targeting early 2027 completion.
stories.summary: UrbanStreet Group is converting a 360,200-square-foot six-story office building—the former Motorola headquarters—into approximately 300 apartment units as part of Schaumburg's $1 billion District at Veridian mixed-use development. The conversion includes a sister four-story building, 18 row houses, retail space, parking, and a 150-key hotel. UrbanStreet secured a $94 million construction loan from Bank OZK in 2025 for the first phase, budgeted at $175 million, targeting early 2027 completion.
stories.impact: Large-scale suburban office-to-residential conversion in prime Illinois market signals strong absorption for new units in walkable, mixed-use settings outside Chicago. District at Veridian's multi-phase approach (first phase approaching delivery) will test suburban renter demand and pricing; strong early absorption could validate suburban conversion thesis beyond dense urban cores. Developers should monitor lease-up velocity and rents; early success could unlock conversion pipeline across Midwest aging office parks.

stories.title: As Deerfield loosens waterfront development rules, West Palm tightens them
stories.excerpt: Two South Florida coastal cities are moving in opposite directions on waterfront development, as localities grapple with regulating construction on the region’s most valuable real estate.  Deerfield Beach is considering looser guidelines for building on the waterfront, while West Palm Beach has temporarily reined in development.  Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 Tuesday for charter amendments that would loosen decadeslong restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and the Atlantic Ocean, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The change also would lift limits on mixed-use developments and projects with accessory use that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Deerfield Beach votes 3-2 to loosen waterfront development rules; eliminates FAR caps on commercial/multifamily between Intracoastal and Atlantic; Nov. 3 referendum required.
West Palm Beach tightens waterfront development rules; two Florida coastal cities diverging on density and regulation.
Deerfield move aims to unlock blighted beachfront parcels while preserving low-rise village character; height/lot coverage limits remain.
stories.summary: Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 to loosen decades-long waterfront development restrictions, including eliminating floor-area-ratio caps for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and Atlantic Ocean. The measure requires final approval on August 4 and voter sign-off in a November 3 referendum. Concurrently, West Palm Beach has tightened waterfront development rules, reflecting divergent coastal policy approaches in South Florida.
stories.impact: Deerfield Beach regulatory shift creates optionality for higher-density, mixed-use waterfront development in a constrained South Florida coastal market. If approved, developers could unlock density on underutilized beachfront parcels, increasing competitive supply pressure on West Palm Beach and Fort Lauderdale waterfront rents. Multifamily developers should track Deerfield referendum outcome; approval would signal regulatory openness to density in secondary coastal markets and create acquisition opportunities in blighted parcels priced for legacy FAR caps.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning, Housing Trust Group net $55.4M financing for 115-unit Artisan Pointe affordable housing in Naranja, south Miami-Dade; completion Jan. 2028.
Package: $31.5M LIHTC equity + $16.9M permanent mortgage (Berkadia) + $7M CRA funds (half tax-increment); rents capped at 30–70% AMI.
Rents: one-bed at 30% AMI = $766/mo; two-bed at 70% AMI = $2,145/mo; below market avg rent of $2,277 in South Florida.
stories.summary: Alonzo Mourning (AM Affordable Housing) and Housing Trust Group secured a $55.4 million financing package for the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project in Naranja, Miami-Dade County. Financing includes $31.5 million in LIHTC equity, a $16.9 million permanent loan from Berkadia, and $7 million from the Naranja Lakes Community Redevelopment Agency (half tax-increment, half loan). Rents will serve households at 30–70% AMI, with one-bedrooms at 30% AMI priced at $766/month and two-bedrooms at 70% AMI at $2,145/month, well below the South Florida median of $2,277.
stories.impact: Strong LIHTC-plus-CRA financing mix demonstrates viable subsidy structure for deep-affordability development in underserved South Miami-Dade markets. Mourning and Housing Trust Group's track record in affordable housing is creating institutional confidence and repeat-financing relationships. Multifamily developers pursuing LIHTC or workforce housing in South Florida should model similar stacking (equity, permanent debt, CRA/TIF) to maximize feasibility; expect increased institutional competition for LIHTC allocations as capital targets affordable supply gaps.
